course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.assumed_background: LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent) is a pre-requisite for LAWS3101. As such, students will be expected to have an understanding of the Australian legal system, the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning to legal problems, and an understanding of the core differences between sole trader businesses, partnerships and companies particularly in so far as ownership rights go.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Lectures - Timetabled

Lectures are timetabled and are conducted live on zoom. A recording of lectures will be made available to students on Blackboard.

Tutorials - Timetabled

Students must sign on to a Tutorial Class and attend the Tutorial Class they are signed on to. Tutorials are scheduled for ten specific weeks during the semester as set out in the week-by-week program in section 4 of the course ECP. Tutorial classes are designed to support student learning by working through student answers to tutorial questions, reflecting on the approaches that should be taken to solve tax-related legal problems and importantly, to focus on the technique required to include specific sections, divisions, case law and ATO Rulings in answers to meet the very specific assessment criteria in LAWS3101. Summative assessment include Tutorial Class preparation marks and students will be expected to submit written answers to tutorials using a flexible approach.

Tutorials are not recorded. Model answers to tutorial questions are not released.

In the first instance, students preference the tutorial class they want to attend that fits in with their timetable and personal circumstances so that they have the opportunity to maximise the benefits of their tutorials. Students should attend the tutorial they are signed on to.

Students are expected to prepare their own answers to tutorial questions to become familiar with the correct approach to answer questions in LAWS3101. During tutorials, we discuss the model answers to questions allowing students ample opportunity to correct their own answers and to ask questions.

There is a range of face-to-face and online tutorials available. Students may attend more than one tutorial to reflect on their own answers, if necessary, after they attended the tutorial they are signed on to. Students who miss their timetabled tutorial due to a medical condition may attend any other tutorial to reflect on their own answers.

In both cases, students who are signed-on to that tutorial must be given the first opportunity to ask their questions. It is not necessary to seek permission from the course coordinator to attend these tutorials.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

The aim of LAWS3101 is to equip you with comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of Australian Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax so that you can solve tax-focused problems in reference to statute law and case law in a workplace setting.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.

Critically evaluate, interpret, apply and incorporate specific provisions of statute law, case law and ATO Rulings in written arguments and in calculations to solve legal problems related to the income tax equation and taxes payable.
Demonstrate a sound understanding of the operation of Goods and Services Tax including its interaction with Income Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax.
Demonstrate a sound understanding of the operation of Fringe Benefits Tax including its interaction with Income Tax and Goods and Services Tax.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of income tax law.

assessment_summary.title: Tutorial work to demonstrate tutorial preparation
assessment_summary.due_date: See due dates and times in ECP section 5.5

assessment_details.title: Tutorial work to demonstrate tutorial preparation
assessment_details.due_date: See due dates and times in ECP section 5.5
assessment_details.task_description:
For each tutorial class, students will be expected to prepare their own answers to each of the questions in each tutorial that must each meet the assessment criteria for the course. Therefore, students must use the topic learning guides that specify the sections, divisions, case law and ATO Rulings that must be referenced in answers to meet the assessment criteria; answers must provide the relevant explanations, discussion and must show all workings.

Tutorial classes are designed to support student learning by working through student answers to tutorial questions, reflecting on the approaches that should be taken to solve tax-related legal problems and importantly, to focus on the technique required to include specific sections, divisions, case law and ATO Rulings, as detailed in the topic learning guides, in answers to meet this very specific assessment criteria in LAWS3101.

Tutorials are not recorded and model answers to tutorials are not released because:

In the first instance, students preference the tutorial class they want to attend that fits in with their timetable and personal circumstances so that they have the opportunity to attend the tutorial of their own choice to maximise the benefits of their tutorials and to check their own answers. As such, students should attend the tutorial they are signed on to.

Students are expected to prepare their own answers to tutorial questions to become familiar with the correct approach to answer questions in LAWS3101. During tutorials, we discuss the model answers to questions allowing students ample opportunity to correct their own answers and to ask questions.

There is a range of face-to-face and online tutorials available. Students may attend more than one tutorial to reflect on their own answers, if necessary, after they attended the tutorial they are signed on to. It is not necessary to seek permission from the course coordinator to do so, but students who are signed-on to this tutorial must be given the first opportunity to ask their questions.

Students who miss their timetabled tutorial due to a medical condition may attend any other tutorial to reflect on their own answers. It is not necessary to seek permission from the course coordinator to do so, but students who are signed-on to this tutorial must be given the first opportunity to ask their questions.

Students who require further feedback about their tutorial answers have several opportunities each week to ask course staff questions during consultation hours or to post their questions on the weekly course discussion boards.

Tutorial assessment:

The maximum total marks available for Tutorials is 15%.

Each individual tutorial submission is worth a maximum of 2.5%. Students will receive a grade of 0, 1% or 2.5% for each tutorial submission. Therefore, students have flexibility in submitting their tutorials to allow for changing student circumstances such as medical conditions and changing personal circumstances.

It is each student’s responsibility to decide how many and which tutorials to submit. A sound approach would be to submit answers to all tutorials to maximise the learning benefits of tutorials and to proactively plan ahead for changing personal circumstances.

These are examples of how a student can achieve the maximum of 15% for tutorials. Example 1: Submit 7 tutorials answering all of the questions in each of the tutorials; and citing sections, divisions, case law and ATO rulings detailed in each topic learning guide, and providing relevant explanations and discussion and all workings in each answer to each question, to earn 2.5% for each submission with the total marks of 17.5% (7 * 2.5%) capped at 15%. Example 2: Submit 8 tutorials, 3 of which do not meet all of the assessment criteria earning 1% each. And submitting 5 tutorials answering all of the questions in each of these tutorials; and citing sections, divisions, case law and ATO rulings detailed in each topic learning guide, and providing relevant explanations and discussion and all workings in each answer to each question, to earn 2.5% for each of these submissions. Total marks = (3 * 1%) + (5 *2.5%) = 15.5% capped at 15%.

Tutorial answer preparation:

Answers must be typed up in a Word document. Only typed up answers will be assessed.

Use a font size between 10 and 12 pt, a font type like Arial or Times new roman, and an A4 size portrait layout.

Type your name and student number on every page in the Header.

Insert page numbers in the Footer.

Save your submission as a Word document.

There is no word limit for tutorial submissions.

Tutorial submission:

Tutorial answers are submitted online as one Word file upload.

Each tutorial has a separate assignment submission link in the Assessment folder for Tutorials on the course site in Blackboard.

There is no limit placed on the total number of tutorials that students can submit, even after they have achieved the total maximum marks for tutorials.

Students can replace any individual tutorial submissions before the due date and time without incurring late submission penalties.

What to do when you miss the tutorial class that you are signed on to, or want to re-check your answers:

There is a range of face-to-face and online tutorials available. Students may attend more than one tutorial to reflect on their own answers, if necessary, after they attended the tutorial they are signed on to. Students who miss their timetabled tutorial due to a medical condition may attend any other tutorial to reflect on their own answers. In both cases, students who are signed-on to that tutorial must be given the first opportunity to ask their questions. It is not necessary to seek permission from the course coordinator to attend these tutorials. As the physical capacity in tutorial rooms is limited, and due to Occupational Health and Safety requirements, room capacity in face-to-face on-campus tutorials cannot be exceeded.

Due dates and times:

Tutorials provide students with a flexible approach to help them navigate their own changing student circumstances. As a result, applications for extension will be rare.

Late submission penalties apply to all tutorial submissions.

These are the due dates and times for each tutorial submission:

Tutorial 1 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 7 March 2022.

Tutorial 2 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 14 March 2022.

Tutorial 3 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 21 March 2022.

Tutorial 4 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 28 March 2022.

Tutorial 5 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 4 April 2022.

Tutorial 6 submission is due by 9 am on Tuesday 3 May 2022.

Tutorial 7 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 9 May 2022.

Tutorial 8 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 16 May 2022.

Tutorial 9 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 23 May 2022.

Tutorial 10 submission is due by 9 am on Monday 30 May 2022.

assessment_details.title: Mid Semester Exam (online timed exam)
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid Semester Exam covers Topic 1, Topic 2, Topic 3, Topic 4 and Topic 5.

The Mid Semester Exam is a timed online exam that starts at 2:15 pm and ends at 3:25 pm (Brisbane time zone) that includes 10 minutes of planning time. This is an individual assessment activity. Students must work alone.

The Mid Semester Exam is an open book exam. However, as this is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the Mid Semester Exam is set on that basis. T herefore, in setting the exam, no time allowance is made for students to look up the answers.

The Mid Semester Exam consists of a number of questions that may include multiple-choice questions, short answer questions, short essay questions, practical calculation questions, discussion questions and problem solving questions. Students are not required to follow the ILAC approach in answering questions (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude). Students must answer all of the questions in the exam in an online test tool. All students start the Mid Semester Exam at the same time.

Where a student reports the inability to complete an exam due to significant technical problems, then that student maybe offered a re-sit or be advised to apply for a deferred exam. The student should supply some evidence of the technical difficulty, for example screenshots and a statement of events. The decision to allocate a re-sit, and if the same paper can be used in a re-sit, is at the discretion of the Course Coordinator who must consult with the Teaching and Learning Chair and the Associate Dean (Academic).

assessment_details.title: Online Invigilated Final Exam
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Extended essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Exam covers Topic 3, Topic 4, Topic 5, Topic 6, Topic 7, Topic 8, Topic 9 and Topic 10.

The Final Exam is a central timed invigilated online exam. This is an individual assessment activity. Students must work alone.

The Final Exam is an open book exam. However, as this is a timed exam, students are expected to know all of the work covered in the exam and the Final Exam is set on that basis. Therefore, in setting the exam, no time allowance is made for students to look up the answers.

The Final Exam consists of a number of questions that may include short answer questions, short essay questions, practical calculation questions, discussion questions and problem solving questions. Students are not required to follow the ILAC approach in answering questions (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude).

Students will be required to prepare all of their answers to the exam in a single document that must be uploaded by following the instructions set by UQ Central Examinations.

Where a student reports the inability to complete an exam due to significant technical problems, then that student maybe offered a re-sit or be advised to apply for a deferred exam. The student should supply some evidence of the technical difficulty, for example screenshots and a statement of events. The decision to allocate a re-sit, and if the same paper can be used in a re-sit, is at the discretion of the Course Coordinator who must consult with the Teaching and Learning Chair and the Associate Dean (Academic).

For online invigilated exams: In the event a student does not have a valid UQ student ID card, a student may produce a government issued identification document.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Principles of Taxation Law 2022 Edition . Thomson Reuters. Richard Krever et al. You may purchase the hard copy of this textbook or the E-book. Hard copy: 9780455245751. E-book: 9780455245768.

LAWS3101 Course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard).

See in particular the weekly folders that contain topic learning guides that are essential to the learning activities in LAWS3101.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
The following acts will be referenced during the course:

Income Tax Assessment Act 1997

Income tax Assessment Act 1936

A New Tax System (GST) Act 1999

Fringe Benefits Tax Assessment Act 1986.

These are available online:

Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

ATO, www.ato.gov.au

The relevant parts of the aforementioned legislation will be included in the study guides for the relevant topics, to supplement other learning resources.

Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Income tax law in Australia is rules-based. To meet the assessment criteria in LAWS3101 your answers must reference specific sections and divisions, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings that are listed in the topic learning guides. It is essential that you consult each topic’s learning guide to know:

Which specific paragraphs from Principles of Taxation Law 2022 you are required to study.

Which specific sections and divisions from legislation to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria

Which specific case law citations to include in your answers in assessments to meet the assessment criteria.

Each topic learning guide will include a series of questions and answers to help you to prepare for assessments.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

Tutorial classes provide students with personal weekly opportunities to receive feedback in LAWS3101. To maximise the benefits of tutorials, students are expected to prepare their own answers to tutorial questions, to attend their tutorial classes and to ask questions.

Students receive a weekly course newsletter to provide them with reflections on common learning problems and how they can solve these.

Each topic learning guide includes a series of questions and answers to help students to determine if they can meet the specific assessment criteria in LAWS3101.

A detailed student feedback document, including model answers, will be released for the Mid Semester Exam and Final Exam.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with Section 3.4 of PPL3.10.02 Assessment ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 3.8 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

course_introduction:
Income tax law is a complex and continually changing area of the law. The overall aim of LAWS3101 is to provide students with an understanding of the fundamentals of the taxation system in Australia and a comprehensive introduction to three specific areas of income tax law and how these areas interact with each other. These are:

Income Tax, including an analysis of the concepts of assessable income, allowable deductions, capital gains, and the calculation of income taxes and other levies which are based on taxable income

Goods and Services Tax

Fringe Benefits Tax.

LAWS3101 takes a practical approach to the law and prepares students for the work-related practices they will follow to solve legal problems using statute law, case law and Australian Taxation Office (ATO) Rulings. To that end, each topic will cover specific sections and divisions of statute law, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings that are essential elements of answers in assessments in order to meet the assessment criteria as described in the topic learning guides. The course does not cover the actual preparation of income tax returns and business activity statements.

Incorporating authentic work-related practices into teaching and assessment

Income tax is an integral part of every finance professional’s life. Income tax law:

Impacts cash flow management

Is a key driver in investment- and business-decisions

Ought to be addressed as part of good governance and in risk management practices, and

Involves timeous compliance and record-keeping.

In order to prepare graduates for these challenges in the workplace, teaching and assessment follow a practical problem-based case study approach. The majority of assessments are time-based, as students will be expected to solve legal problems time-efficiently in the workplace.

Rules-based course content and assessment criteria

Income tax law in Australia is rules-based. Therefore, students must know, apply and include specific section and division numbers, specific case law and specific ATO Rulings in their answers in assessments in order to meet the assessment criteria. As it is not possible to cover all of the sections, divisions, case law and rulings that make up income tax law in this course, it is essential that students consult each topic’s learning guide to know which specific sections, divisions, case law and rulings they must study, apply and specifically incorporate in their answers to meet the assessment criteria. Students should plan to invest significant time so that they are able to demonstrate their mastery of the detailed rules embedded in these specific sections and divisions of statute law, specific case law and ATO Rulings and to be able to incorporate these into their answers.

Accreditation

LAWS3101 Income Tax Law is accredited with Chartered Accountants Australia New Zealand and CPA Australia. Students who pass the course are able to enrol for the taxation modules in the final professional exams of these two professional bodies on their pathway to qualify as chartered accountants. As a result, the breadth and depth of work covered in the course is significant, and assessment methods mirror what students can expect in these professional exams. Students who plan on sitting other professional exams will gain valuable experience in preparing for these by successfully completing LAWS3101.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law

The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize. One prize will be awarded. This prize is not shared between students.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

In order to provide students with more personal feedback and to enhance their opportunities to practice the technique to include sections, divisions, case law and ATO Rulings in answers to meet the very specific assessment criteria in LAWS3101, assessment will include marks for class preparation. To that end, students will be expected to submit written answers to tutorials as part of their summative assessment. These assessment marks replace the income tax case study that formed part of summative assessment in previous semesters. The weighting of assessments changes accordingly.

The Final Exam will be conducted as on online invigilated exam. The details of the invigilation process and software requirements will be provided to students when these become available. An online invigilated final practice exam is planned so that students can become familiar with this form of assessment (subject to change). A component of invigilated summative assessments is part of the accreditation requirements for the course set by the relevant professional bodies.

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and the lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

In a negative sense, lectures are not for:

Delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn,

Reading material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of Tutorial Classes

Tutorial Classes offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the tutorial staff and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the Tutorial Classes are dependent upon all students having prepared answers to the tutorial questions and being prepared to engage in debate and to ask questions.

Topic Learning guides

The Topic Learning guides are essential to students' success in LAWS3101. Students must use these essential and important course resources to reduce the hours they spend on the course work each week.

Weekly course newsletter and discussion boards

Students must read the weekly course newsletter and student questions with course staff answers to these in the weekly discussion boards.

Additional student learning support

Over the course of the semester, students will have the opportunity to check their knowledge by completing optional online quizzes and practice exams that will not be graded, as well as to watch course videos that will help them to meet the course assessment criteria.

Online invigilated final practice exam

To meet the accreditation criteria of professional bodies the Final Exam will be conducted as an online invigilated timed exam. The Law School will endeavour to set up an online invigilated final practice exam - details to follow on the course site when these become available.

course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +
late_submission:
Penalties for Late Submission

Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission. Assessment submitted more than 7 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 2pm on Monday but before 2pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 2pm on the 17th will receive zero marks.

Extension to Assessment Due Date

Extensions will not normally exceed 14 days. If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 14 days, we advise you to consider carefully whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might be eligible to withdraw without academic penalty.

Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.

Reasons for extension and evidence :

• If the basis of the application is a medical condition:

o Applications must be accompanied by a medical certificate dated on or before the assignment due date.

o It is your responsibility to ensure that the provider of your medical certificate is a genuine medical practitioner. You will be held fully responsible for whatever you submit, even if you do so in apparent ignorance. It is therefore your responsibility to ensure that anything you submit is authentic.

o An extension granted to a student on medical grounds will not exceed the number of days suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate. The medical condition must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment.

• If the basis of the application is some other circumstance:

o The circumstances must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

o the extension granted will be in proportion to the period of disruption caused by the circumstances, and be no more than two weeks in duration.

Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply .

Applications to be submitted before the due date : Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date will not be accepted unless there is evidence to demonstrate extenuating circumstances that made it impossible to submit the request earlier. Late requests received by the School will require the decision maker listed in section 2.3.2 of PPL 3.60.02 Student Grievance Resolution to accept the request for consideration.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other

To meet the assessment criteria in all of the assessments, students must:

Apply the requirements detailed in each topic learning guide that specifies which specific sections and division numbers, which case law, and which ATO Rulings must be included in answers; and

Provide the necessary reasons, explanations and descriptions in their answers; and

Show all their calculations.
